summary: The meeting focused on research progress updates, particularly around speech recognition systems, including the TORRENT chip, Aurora baseline system, and various experiments with spectral subtraction, VAD (Voice Activity Detection), and mean subtraction techniques. The main purpose was to discuss individual research updates, troubleshoot experimental results, and plan next steps.

Professor B: Expressed frustration about the TORRENT chip project being perpetually 'two months away from completion' for two years
Suggested that Grad E should reverse previous phase normalization experiments to potentially improve results
Questioned the practical importance of VAD improvements beyond academic interest
Advocated for combining energy and zero-crossing methods for better VAD performance
Noted that SRI system uses more parameters and finer-grained acoustic models than HTK
PhD A: Suggested implementing a status report format for meetings
Questioned whether Grad E added mean-subtracted data to the Aurora training set or only tested with it
Proposed using the full Aurora test with SRI system instead of just TI-digits
Suggested looking at the derivative of energy signals to detect speech beginnings
Asked if the Aurora test should include all languages for proper comparison
Grad F: Described his proposal for iterative intermediate category classification starting with phonetic features
Planned to use TIMIT for initial phoneme recognition and Switchboard for cross-phoneme modeling
Mentioned completing a section on intermediate categories for his proposal
Noted his quals are scheduled for July 25th
Expressed concern about getting his own features into SRI files
Grad E: Reported 6% to 4.5% WER improvement on far-mike data but worsening near-mike performance with mean subtraction
Theorized that SRI system's channel adaptation and finer models make it more sensitive to re-synthesis artifacts
Tried phase normalization by taking mean of FFT spectrum but found it didn't work
Planned to train on Macrophone dataset to improve robustness to artifacts
Questioned whether mean subtraction acts as gain control for quiet PZM signals
PhD C: Investigated VAD performance showing it drops few frames for certain noise types at low SNR
Started experiments with Makhoul-type spectral subtraction using different over-estimation factors
Suggested testing on TI-digits first to understand Macrophone training improvements
Proposed combining energy-based VAD with MLP decisions
Questioned whether noise estimation should happen before or after on-line normalization
PhD G: Questioned the type of VAD that will be provided (realistic vs manual segmentation)
Noted that DC components in microphones are typically removed by capacitors
Suggested using energy derivative to detect speech beginnings
Emphasized that all systems have delays requiring buffering
Questioned whether spectral subtraction should be done in cepstral domain
PhD D: Suggested estimating noise with first frames before applying VAD
Proposed applying VAD before on-line normalization
Expressed interest in working on noise estimation and normalization order

agreements.details: Agreed to wait for Sunil to arrive before discussing his status
Agreed that starting with reasonable intermediate features like phonetic features is a good approach
Agreed that Grad E should try training on a larger dataset (Macrophone) to improve robustness
Agreed that combining energy and zero-crossing methods could improve VAD performance
Agreed to start with smaller tests before attempting full Aurora evaluation
Agreed that Grad E should reverse previous phase normalization attempts
Agreed to experiment with different over-estimation factors and added noise values
Agreed that noise estimation should potentially happen before on-line normalization

disagreements.details: Disagreement on why mean subtraction improved far-mike but worsened near-mike performance
Disagreement on whether taking mean of phase spectrum or FFT spectrum is the correct approach
Disagreement on whether VAD improvements matter beyond academic interest
Disagreement on whether to test with just TI-digits or full Aurora system with all languages
Disagreement on whether to perform spectral subtraction in time/frequency domain or cepstral domain
Disagreement on whether mean subtraction acts as gain control for quiet signals
Disagreement on how to best combine energy-based VAD with MLP decisions
Disagreement on the significance of DC components in microphone recordings

most_time_consuming.topic: mean subtraction experiments and results interpretation
most_time_consuming.reason: The discussion consumed significant time analyzing why mean subtraction improved far-mike performance (6% to 4.5% WER) but worsened near-mike performance (1.2% to 2.4%), with multiple theories about channel adaptation, model sensitivity, re-synthesis artifacts, and potential gain control effects being debated.

summary: The Children, Young People and Education Committee held a scrutiny session focusing on higher and further education, with discussions on teacher training recruitment, Welsh-medium education, post-16 education reforms, funding, parity of esteem between academic and vocational education, and concerns about essay writing services.

Kirsty Williams AM: Teacher recruitment is an international issue requiring a multi-faceted approach including financial incentives, marketing, and flexible training routes.
Welsh-medium teacher training needs a step change to meet targets, with new incentives and interventions being implemented.
Post-16 education reforms aim to create a commission promoting collaboration rather than competition between institutions.
University mergers should be institution-led, not government-driven, with focus on student benefit.
Essay writing services undermine academic integrity and require a UK-wide approach to regulation.
Eluned Morgan AM: Building confidence in Welsh speakers to teach through the medium of Welsh is crucial, requiring support and identification of potential teachers.
Higher apprenticeships need improvement in employer engagement and course relevance to employer needs.
Funding for apprenticeships should focus on skills shortage areas like ICT and construction.
Private providers of apprenticeships require better quality assurance and guidance.
State investment in apprenticeships must balance with employer responsibility to avoid shifting costs.
Huw Morris: Fee and access plans for universities are monitored by HEFCW to ensure equality of opportunity.
Data from HESA and UCAS is used to track access and progression, supplemented by Welsh-specific analysis.
Reconfiguration of mergers, like University of South Wales, is being evaluated to inform future policy.
A new apprenticeship procurement process will drive change in the system.
UK-wide coordination is needed to address essay writing services.
Suzy Davies AM: Teacher training recruitment trends need better research to understand why people are deterred from the profession.
Part-time PGCE routes could bring valuable outside experience into teaching.
Welsh language skills in teacher trainees need to be maintained beyond school education.
Geographical accessibility of teacher training courses impacts recruitment.
Funding cuts to part-time provision could undermine the Diamond reforms.
Llyr Gruffydd AM: The decline in Welsh-medium teacher training is 'cataclysmic' and requires more ambitious action.
Post-16 education reforms need a clear vision to effect cultural change, not just structural changes.
Funding cuts to part-time teaching could harm the success of the Diamond reforms.
University mergers should be approached cautiously due to potential disruption.
The new commission must ensure true parity of esteem for FE and HE.

agreements.details: Recognized as an international issue requiring multiple strategies including financial incentives, marketing, and flexible training routes.
Need for a step change to meet targets, with new incentives and interventions being implemented.
Aim to create a commission promoting collaboration rather than competition between institutions.
Should be institution-led, not government-driven, with focus on student benefit.
Undermine academic integrity and require a UK-wide approach to regulation.
Need improvement in employer engagement and course relevance to employer needs.
Should focus on skills shortage areas like ICT and construction.
Require better quality assurance and guidance.

disagreements.details: Disagreement on which statistics to use for setting targets, with Llyr Gruffydd questioning the baseline and interpretation.
Llyr Gruffydd felt there was a vacuum in vision, while Kirsty Williams disagreed, stating the vision had been consulted on.
Hefin David expressed concerns about disruption and sustainability, while Kirsty Williams maintained an agnostic stance.
Llyr Gruffydd raised concerns about cuts impacting the Diamond reforms, while Kirsty Williams was not concerned at the time.
Hefin David questioned the value of the University of South Wales merger, while Kirsty Williams deferred to past decisions.
Julie Morgan questioned the lack of investment, while Eluned Morgan emphasized balancing state and employer responsibility.
Hefin David called for outlawing websites, while Kirsty Williams stressed the need for UK-wide coordination.
Llyr Gruffydd felt the targets were not ambitious enough, while Kirsty Williams acknowledged the challenge but defended the approach.

most_time_consuming.reason: The discussion covered multiple aspects including recruitment challenges, Welsh-medium training decline, statistics interpretation, and potential solutions, with extensive back-and-forth between Kirsty Williams and committee members.

summary: The meeting was an evidence session with the Welsh Government regarding the impact of the coronavirus pandemic on health and social services for children and young people in Wales. The discussion covered topics including children's transmission of the virus, healthcare capacity, mental health support, safeguarding, childcare provision, and third sector funding.

Vaughan Gething AM: Children are less likely to be significantly affected by COVID-19 than older people, but their role in transmission is still not fully understood.
Mental health and well-being of children is a central concern and remains so for both health and education ministers.
We need to rebuild confidence in health services to ensure parents engage with care for their children.
The mental health recovery plan will be of real importance to address potential long-term impacts on children.
Sian Gwenllian AM: There is concern about the impact of the pandemic on children's mental health and well-being, and the lack of full assessment of this impact.
Childcare providers are falling through the cracks in financial support, risking sector collapse.
There is a need for a bespoke support package for childcare providers not currently receiving aid.
Julie Morgan AM: The Welsh Government is working closely with the third sector to support children and young people during the pandemic.
There has been a drop in safeguarding referrals to social services, which is a concern.
The definition of vulnerable children has been expanded to include those on the edge of care.
Tracey Breheny: CAMHS services are currently meeting capacity needs, with weekly monitoring in place.
Some CAMHS staff were shifted to other roles during the initial phase of the pandemic but are now returning.
Professor Jean White: Routine immunisations saw a downturn initially but attendance is now improving through outreach efforts.

agreements.details: Free childcare is provided for critical workers and vulnerable children aged 0-5, with around 1,500 children accessing this support.
Health visiting, immunisation, and perinatal mental health services continue to be provided during the pandemic.

Weekly meetings with local authorities and data collection are in place to monitor children's services.

summary: The meeting focused on the Welsh Government's childcare offer, discussing its implementation progress, challenges, and future plans. The Minister for Children and Social Care, Huw Irranca-Davies, provided updates on pilot areas, administrative burdens, and funding, while committee members raised concerns about affordability, workforce capacity, and equity.

Huw Irranca-Davies AM: The childcare offer is progressing well through early implementer areas, with lessons learned about administrative burdens and communication
A universal £4.50 hourly rate is preferred for simplicity, though adjustments may be needed for high-cost areas
The scheme focuses on registered providers to ensure quality education and socialisation, not just childcare

summary: The Children, Young People and Education Committee held an evidence session on the financial sustainability and governance of higher education institutions in Wales, focusing on funding allocations, financial challenges, and sector oversight.

Dr David Blaney: Welsh universities face significant financial challenges due to demographic dips, increased costs, and Brexit uncertainty, but are managing rather than in crisis.
The Diamond reforms are crucial for improving university funding, though the sector remains £40 million worse off than English institutions.
Universities are cutting core capacity, not just 'fat', which will diminish curriculum range and research contributions.
Governance in Welsh universities is generally effective, though external oversight from HEFCW helps ensure constructive challenge.
There is no immediate risk of university collapse, but ongoing financial pressures could lead to more job losses and capacity reduction.
Bethan Owen: University reserves are often tied up in estates, with operating cash generation (7.6% in 2017-18) lower than in England (10%).
Pension cost increases and income stagnation pose significant challenges to university financial forecasts.
HEFCW monitors universities closely, requiring five-year forecasts and scenario planning to assess financial risks.
Trinity Saint David's material uncertainty stems from delayed Egin project funding, not governance failures.
Part-time student funding is growing but requires careful prioritization under current arrangements.
Angela Burns AM: Universities were previously criticized for hoarding reserves instead of investing in students; the current picture shows reduced liquidity.
Trinity Saint David's financial plans include material uncertainty, raising concerns about course sustainability for students.
University borrowing for estates must be prudently managed, with HEFCW oversight ensuring covenant compliance.
Media portrayal of a 'crisis' could harm student recruitment, despite Wales having the best student satisfaction in the UK.
Governance arrangements need consistent rigor across institutions to address financial risks.
Hefin David AM: Governance cultures vary across institutions, but HEFCW aims to establish consistent minimum expectations.
Risk appetite is generally prudent, though HEFCW occasionally helps institutions reassess overly optimistic plans.
Part-time student growth is unsustainable without increased funding, given price sensitivity and lifelong learning demands.
Research funding (QR) has been stagnant for nine years, limiting Welsh universities' competitive capacity.
The direction of travel shows increasing risk profiles, with more universities potentially moving to high-risk categories.
Sian Gwenllian AM: Stagnant QR funding undermines Welsh research capacity and economic impact, despite the sector's productivity.
Educational research requires dedicated funding, though HEFCW has supported WISERD Education collaboratively.
Brexit poses a severe threat to research funding, requiring urgent investment and UK-wide engagement.
Innovation funding could help universities access UKRI pots, but sustainable resources are needed.
Transparency around fee income use is essential to understand cross-subsidization between teaching and research.

agreements.details: Universities face demographic dips, pension cost increases, and Brexit uncertainty, leading to managed deficits rather than crisis.
HEFCW monitors universities through forecasts, risk reviews, and borrowing consent, ensuring prudent financial management.
Universities are contractually obligated to complete courses for existing students, with HEFCW quality assurance machinery ensuring this.
£1.9 million additional funding in 2019-20 supports part-time student growth, but sustainability requires future prioritization.
QR funding has remained static for nine years, limiting Welsh universities' ability to compete for UK and international grants.
Loss of EU funds requires urgent investment and UK-wide engagement to maintain research capacity.
Universities have committed £28 million to student support, including bursaries for accommodation and living costs.
Material uncertainty stems from delayed Egin project funding, not governance failures, with new covenants renegotiated.

disagreements.details: Committee members pressed for disclosure of which universities are high/medium/low risk, but HEFCW declined to publish specifics.
Angela Burns questioned whether universities had shifted from hoarding reserves to investing in students, while HEFCW emphasized reserve complexities.
Blaney warned against media amplification of financial challenges, while Angela Burns argued scrutiny was necessary for transparency.
Hefin David sought assurance on consistent governance approaches, while Blaney noted cultural differences across institutions.
Hefin David questioned potential fee hikes due to price sensitivity, while Blaney suggested self-regulation limits would prevent significant increases.
Sian Gwenllian pressed for dedicated 2019-20 allocations, while Blaney indicated funding was still under consideration.
Sian Gwenllian called for HEFCW's own impact assessment of underfunded research, while Blaney relied on external expert reports.
Bethan Owen highlighted £15 million in proposed innovation funding, but Sian Gwenllian questioned its adequacy without sustainable resources.

most_time_consuming.reason: The discussion covered multiple complex issues, including financial indicators, reserves, borrowing covenants, risk categorization, and governance oversight, requiring detailed explanations and back-and-forth between committee members and witnesses.
